coredata.dc:title: Construction of novel Z-scheme CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP heterojunction for photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Jing
Zhang, Yaqian
Wang, Bowen
Zhang, Fenge
coredata.dc:description: Bi4O5Br2 material exhibits excellent performance in photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics, but high recombination rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs limits its photocatalytic activity. Attapulgite (ATP) is an excellent candidate for load type catalyst due to its larger specific surface area. As a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have important application prospects in catalysis and other fields. Herein, Z-scheme CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite photocatalyst loaded with varied proportions of CDs was synthesized by dynamic adsorption method, which demonstrated excellent photocatalytic degradation performance by lowering the recombination rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Ciprofloxacin degradation efficiency by 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP can reach up to 90% within 120 min under simulated sunlight, in addition, the 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite photocatalyst has good stability. As a result, our research helps to prepare high-efficiency Z-scheme heterojunction and contributes to study the degradation of antibiotics in the aquatic environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi4O5Br2 material
Ciprofloxacin
CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite photocatalyst
Z-scheme heterojunction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance of photocatalytic degradation of CIP by CDs/ Bi4O5Br2/ATP
Active species capture experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the electron transfer process of Z-scheme.
XRD patterns of ATP, Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5Br2/ATP, CDs and 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite.
FT-IR spectra of ATP, Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5Br2/ATP, CDs and 5 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP.
TEM images of (a)pure CDs, (b) pure Bi4O5Br2, (c)Bi4O5Br2/ATP, (d)5 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP.

(a) Solid UV of pure Bi4O5Br2, pure ATP, Bi4O5Br2/ATP, and CDs/ Bi4O5Br2/ATP composites with a series of carbon dots loading ratios;(b) Band gap energy of pure Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5Br2/ATP and CDs/ Bi4O5Br2/ATP composites with a range of carbon dots loading ratios (inset is the band gap energy of pure ATP).
XPS spectra of ATP, Bi4O5Br2/ATP and 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite (a) Full spectrum of the sample, (b) Bi 4f, (c) Br 3d, (d) Fe 2p, (e) Si 2p, (f) O 1 s, (g) C 1 s.
(a) Degradation rate curves of CIP under visible light and simulated sunlight; (b) Pseudo-first-order kinetic curve of CIP degradation.
(a) Cycling experiment of CIP degradation by 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP; (b)XRD analysis of 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP before and after 4 times catalytic degradation of CIP.
Fluorescence spectral analysis of Bi4O5Br2, ATP, Bi4O5Br2/ATP and 3 wt% CDs/ Bi4O5Br2/ATP at 300 nm excitation wavelength.
（a）Free radical trapping experiment;（b）The ESR active species map under visible light.
（a）Free radical trapping experiment;（b）The ESR active species map under simulated sunlight.
CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP mechanism diagram of photocatalytic degradation of CIP(a) visible light; (b), (c) simulated sunlight.

head.ce:title: Construction of novel Z-scheme CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP heterojunction for photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Urban Construction, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PRChina
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Urban Construction, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PRChina

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Changzhou Drainage Management Office, Changzhou 213017, PRChina
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Changzhou Drainage Management Office, Changzhou 213017, PRChina

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bi4O5Br2 material exhibits excellent performance in photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics, but high recombination rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs limits its photocatalytic activity. Attapulgite (ATP) is an excellent candidate for load type catalyst due to its larger specific surface area. As a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have important application prospects in catalysis and other fields. Herein, Z-scheme CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite photocatalyst loaded with varied proportions of CDs was synthesized by dynamic adsorption method, which demonstrated excellent photocatalytic degradation performance by lowering the recombination rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Ciprofloxacin degradation efficiency by 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP can reach up to 90% within 120 min under simulated sunlight, in addition, the 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite photocatalyst has good stability. As a result, our research helps to prepare high-efficiency Z-scheme heterojunction and contributes to study the degradation of antibiotics in the aquatic environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging environmental problem, more and more antibiotics have been discovered in various aquatic settings, such as surface water, groundwater, and wastewater from sewage treatment plant in recent years [1,2,3]. Quinolone antibiotics, such as ciprofloxacin (CIP), are major risk factors and pollution factors in Chinese lakes, and are hazardous to non-target species. Continuous discharge into the aquatic ecosystem will have negative ecological consequences [4,5,6], and have the potential to be very harmful to aquatic species [7]. There is an urgent need to develop an ecologically friendly approach to effectively remove CIP from aqueous solution, and photocatalysis is thought to be a suitable method [8,9,10,11]. Photocatalytic oxidation technology has attracted widespread attention in pollutant removal due to its strong oxidizing ability, good environmental compatibility, and low cost [12,13,14,15,16]. To rapidly react with and eliminate organic contaminants, photocatalytic oxidation technology employs the high oxidizing power of hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals.

Because it responds strongly to visible light and has an adequate band gap location, the Bi4O5Br2 material exhibits outstanding photocatalytic activity [17,18]. While, Bi4O5Br2 still has a high recombination rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs due to the fact that Bi, O and Br are not single stoichiometric ratio, which restricts the photocatalytic activity [19,20]. Improving the photocatalytic performance of Bi4O5Br2 has emerged as a research priority.

Bi4O5Br2 is a typical bismuth-rich polyoxygen semiconductor material that responds to visible light. Bi4O5Br2 crystal structure is layered, comparable to BiOBr crystal structure. The bromide ion layer (Br-) and the bismuth oxide layer ([Bi2O2]2+) are alternately arranged along the (001) direction, and these two arrangements in the lattice can generate the internal electric field, causing the IEF to be perpendicular to the two-dimensional layered structure. Bi4O5Br2′s recently stated prohibited band width is Eg = 2.19～2.54 eV, and its conduction band bottom is more negative than that of BiOBr. Because of the lower conduction band location, molecular oxygen may be efficiently activated to create ·O2 – active groups, resulting in increased catalytic activity. Nonetheless, visible light’s limited usage restricts its practical applicability. As a result, it is critical to investigate the modification of Bi4O5Br2 and improve its application to actual industrial production.

Bard proposed the Z-scheme photocatalytic system for the first time in 1979 [21]. The electron transfer process of the Z-scheme is depicted schematically in Fig. 1 . Light excites both I and II, producing electrons in CB and holes in VB, respectively. The photogenerated electrons in CB of I are transported to VB of II through a redox mediator, leaving strong oxidation pores in VB of I and strong reducing electrons in CB of II, which participate in the oxidation reaction and reduction reaction on the surface of the photocatalyst, respectively. For one thing, the holes in VB of I react with low-valence ions to produce high-valence ions. For another, the photogenerated electrons in CB of II are consumed by interacting with high-valence ions in redox electron mediators. As a result, the concentration of redox couples remains essentially constant throughout the reaction process.

Because of its abundance of active sites and larger specific surface area, attapulgite (ATP) is an excellent candidate for load type catalyst and absorbent [22]. Furthermore, it has been observed that pure ATP absorbs UV light, making it a UV-responsive semiconductor [23]. Therefore, it is possible to produce a Bi4O5Br2/ATP heterojunction by connecting Bi4O5Br2 with ATP, which allows electrons and holes to be efficiently separated. On the other hand, the more negative conduction band (CB) and the more positive valence band (VB) of the potential have higher reducing and oxidizing abilities [24,25]. The evident disadvantage of typical semiconductor heterojunction is that the redox capacity of photogenerated electrons and holes is diminished following charge transfer. Because the Z-scheme system can efficiently segregate electrons and holes, it has a significant reduction/oxidation capability [26]. ATP solid Z-type photocatalysts have been widely investigated in all Z-scheme systems by employing precious metals (Au, Pt, Ag) particles as electron mediators, but difficult fabrication procedures and higher costs restrict their widespread application [27]. However, carbon dots (CDs) have gained popularity due to their simplicity of manufacture and inexpensive cost [28,29]. CDs have recently been shown to be an excellent solid electron medium in Z-scheme systems, with increased photocatalytic activity for water breakdown and photocatalytic hydrogen production [30,31].

Because of the favorable band structure between CDs, Bi4O5Br2 and ATP, a novel Z-scheme CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP heterojunction could be formed, which could decrease the photoexcited electron-hole pair recombination rate [32,33,34,35], thus improve the photocatalytic performance of Bi4O5Br2.

This study used the dynamic adsorption method to synthesize CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite photocatalytic materials loaded with varied proportions of CDs. Using CIP as the target pollutant, the photodegradation performance was evaluated in simulated sunlight, and the variation rule of photocatalyst activity with different mass ratios was explored to get the ideal composite ratio. Furthermore, the catalyst’s stability and photodegradation process were investigated.

Firstly, a glass plate was put on top of the burning candle to collect candle ash. Secondly, the collected ash was ultrasonically dispersed in 50 mL ammonia solution for 30 min. The suspension was then moved from the beaker to an autoclave for 7 h of hydrothermal treatment before being baked at 150 °C in an oven. Finally, it was washed alternately with ethanol and water for three times before being dried in an oven at 80 °C.

2 mmol potassium bromide (KBr) was ultrasonically dissolved in 20 mL glycerin, as was 2 mmol bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) in 20 mL glycerin. The KBr solution was then added dropwise to the Bi(NO3)3·5H2O solution while being continuously agitated. The suspension was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave lined with teflon and held at 160 °C for 16 h. Following the reaction, the complex precursor precipitate was recovered by centrifugation and washed three times with ethanol and water. It would then be dried at 80 °C.

Disperse the molecular precursor (0.3 g) in 100 mL deionized water. Then, it was hydrolyzed at 50 °C for 24 h and washed with deionized water. At last, it was dried at 80 °C and heat-treated at 200 °C for approximate 3 h to obtain Bi4O5Br2.
A certain amount of ATP (1.0 g) and the prepared Bi4O5Br2 (0.05 g) were stirred at 50 °C for 24 h to obtain Bi4O5Br2/ATP heterojunction.

The prepared CDs and Bi4O5Br2/ATP were stirred at a constant temperature of 50 °C for 24 h to obtain a series of composite photocatalysts with different loading ratios. The weight of the introduced CDs was 1, 3 and 5 wt%, respectively. Expressed as 1 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP, 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP and 5 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP.

The crystal structure of photocatalyst was analyzed by D/MAX 2500 PC automatic rotating target X-ray diffractometer. FT-IR spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 370 spectrometer. The element composition and valence state of photocatalyst were verified by XPS analyzer. The morphologies, size and structure of the photocatalyst were verified by FEI Talos F200X transmission electron microscope. The light absorption properties of the photocatalyst were analyzed by UV–vis spectrophotometer. The photogenerated electron behavior of photocatalyst could be analyzed by fluorescence spectrometer.

A 350 W xenon lamp was utilized as the light source to generate visible light by loading a filter. When circulating cooling water and an electric fan functioned concurrently, the entire photocatalytic process could be carried out under simulated sunshine and visible light at around 30 °C. In a test tube, 50 mL of 10 mg·L−1 CIP solution was poured, and 0.05 g catalyst was added. The entire procedure was carried out in complete darkness. After 30 min of dark reaction to achieve adsorption equilibrium, the photocatalytic degradation process was initiated by turning on the light source. 4 mL samples were taken every 20 min, and the samples were centrifuged at 6000 r/min for 20 min, and the supernatant was stored for detection. Moreover, in order to further evaluate the long-term performance of CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP, CIP degradation test was repeated for 4 times to investigate the optical stability of the catalyst. The CIP concentration of the samples was tested by UV–vis spectrophotometry at λ = 275 nm (each sample was tested for three times and the results were calculated as the average value).

The XRD patterns of ATP, Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5Br2/ATP, CDs, and 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP are shown in Fig. 2 . Each sample’s crystal structure was determined by referring to standard cards in the International Diffraction Data Center database. The diffraction peak at 26.6° of ATP was attributed to SiO2. The XRD pattern of Bi4O5Br2 is compatible with the standard XRD pattern of PDF: 00-037-0699, and it has a conventional monoclinic crystal structure, which is consistent with earlier investigations [36]. The unique peak value of pure CDs at 24° can point to the (101) plane of the graphite structure. However, due to the low crystallinity of CDs, no characteristic peak of carbon was found in the 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite, indicating that CDs is highly dispersed on the surface of Bi4O5Br2/ATP.

The infrared absorption spectra of ATP, Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5Br2/ATP, CDs and 5 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP are shown in Fig. 3 . For pure ATP, the characteristic peak at 3457 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the −OH structure, the absorption bands at 1436 and 1645 cm−1 are caused by the bending vibration of adsorbed water, and the peak of 1002 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibrations of Si (or Al)−O tetrahedra. In pure Bi4O5Br2, the peak at 521 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of the Bi−O bond. It can be found that the characteristic peaks of ATP and Bi4O5Br2 can be identified in the Bi4O5Br2/ATP sample, indicating that Bi4O5Br2 has been successfully loaded on ATP. As for CDs, it contains functional groups such as −OH (3437 cm−1) and −C=O (1690 cm−1). 5 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP sample has the same characteristic peaks as Bi4O5Br2/ATP.

TEM and HRTEM were used to study the morphology of CDs, Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5Br2/ATP and 5 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP. Fig. 4 (a) depicts a typical TEM image of pure CDs particles. The particles are evenly dispersed with an average particle size of about 20 nm. Fig. 4 (b) depicts the TEM image of pure Bi4O5Br2. It can be seen that the lattice spacing of Bi4O5Br2 is 0.302 nm. Fig. 4 (c) shows TEM image of Bi4O5Br2/ATP, where lattice fringes of d = 0.302 nm on the ATP can be observed, which can be ascribed to (113) plane of Bi4O5Br2 [37]. TEM image of CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite is shown in Fig. 4 (d). HRTEM photograph taken from randomly selected location shows that Bi4O5Br2 and CDs have different streaks.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5Br2/ATP, and CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP with varied CDs loading ratios are shown in Fig. 5 (a). It was discovered that pure ATP had the greatest absorption effect in the UV region, whereas pure Bi4O5Br2 had a noticeable adsorption edge in the visible range. When compared to Bi4O5Br2/ATP, the CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite shown considerably better visible light response capabilities, and its absorption edge clearly migrated to a broader wavelength. In theory, as the number of CDs rose, the adsorption edge would broaden [38]. However, when the amount of CDs exceeded 3 wt%, the absorption edge exhibited a strong blue shift. This might be due to an overabundance of CDs causing aggregation, which reduces light absorption. Therefore, appropriate amount of CDs showed good dispersion on Bi4O5Br2/ATP surface, which was conducive to the photocatalytic performance.

The band gap energy spectra of Bi4O5Br2, Bi4O5Br2/ATP, and 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP are shown in Fig. 5 (b), with band gap energies of 2.32, 2.15, and 1.74 eV, respectively. The pure ATP band gap spectrum with a band gap energy of 3.25 eV is depicted in Fig. 5 (b). Based on the UV–vis data, it was possible to deduce that pure ATP could not be stimulated by visible light because its band gap was so enormous that it could not absorb visible light [39]. CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composites demonstrated a larger visible light absorption range than Bi4O5Br2 and Bi4O5Br2/ATP. This was owing to the doped CDs, which lowered the energy required for the nanocomposite’s first electronic transition in visible light and enhanced visible light absorption. Furthermore, 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP demonstrated the greatest absorption edge due to the creation of an interface between ATP and Bi4O5Br2. Thus, interfacial charge transfer through CDs increased UV and visible light absorption.

The XPS spectra of ATP, Bi4O5Br2/ATP and 3 wt% CDs/ Bi4O5Br2/ATP composites are shown in Fig. 6 . The XPS survey spectrum of ATP, Bi4O5Br2/ATP and CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP includes Bi, Br, Fe, Mg, Si, O and C, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The Bi 4f spectrum of the Bi4O5Br2/ATP sample is comprised of two peaks with binding energies of 164.4 eV and 159.0 eV, which respectively correspond to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 orbits of Bi3+, as shown in Fig. 6 (b). The Bi 4f peak in the CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP sample differed somewhat from the Bi 4f peak in the Bi4O5Br2/ATP sample. The results reveal that the chemical environment of Bi on the surface of CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP has altered and the Bi−C interaction may be evaluated in CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP samples. The peak at 68.4 eV in Fig. 6 (c) corresponds to the Br 3d binding energy of Bi4O5Br2/ATP samples. The XPS spectra of Fe 2p of ATP, Bi4O5Br2/ATP and 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composites are shown in Fig. 6 (d). There are two peaks about 711 eV and 724 eV that correspond to the spin–orbit peaks of Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively. The results reveal that the majority of the Fe species in the composite were ferric iron (Fe3+) [40]. A example XPS spectra of Si 2p3/2 centered at 102 eV is shown in Fig. 6 (e). Bi4O5Br2/ATP and CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP had smaller energy shifts than ATP, implying interactions between ATP and other species [41]. O 1s peak at 531 eV of Bi4O5Br2/ATP and CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP in Fig. 6 (f) can be ascribed to O in Bi4O5Br2 crystal. The O 1s peak has a displacement of roughly 0.8 eV compared to pure ATP, resulting in a decreased binding energy, showing an interaction between Bi4O5Br2 and ATP. Fig. 6 (g) depicts an XPS spectrum of C 1s produced from CDs doped on the Bi4O5Br2/ATP. The peaks at 284.82 eV and 286.47 eV correspond to C−C bond or C=C bond of graphite structure of CDs, respectively, whereas the binding energies at 288.11 eV and 289.59 eV may be attributed to C−C bond and C=C bond, respectively [42,43]. As a consequence, CDs binded tightly with Bi4O5Br2/ATP composites, which was consistent with other characterization results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of photocatalytic degradation of CIP by CDs/ Bi4O5Br2/ATP

ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic oxidation of CIP is depicted in Fig. 7 . Within 30 min, the adsorption–desorption equilibrium between catalyst and CIP had been attained (Fig. S1). The change in CIP degrading efficiency with irradiation time is seen in Fig. 7 (a). Photogenic reaction radicals oxidized CIP to carbon dioxide and water. In comparison, Fig. 7 (a) demonstrates CIP degradation by 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP and Bi4O5Br2/ATP when exposed to visible light. Because of its adsorption qualities in CIP aqueous solution and UV response characteristics, pure ATP had a CIP degradation efficiency of about 10%. Obviously, the photocatalytic activity of Bi4O5Br2/ATP was significantly greater than that of pure ATP and Bi4O5Br2. When Bi4O5Br2/ATP was modified by a small amount of CDs, the CIP degradation efficiency could be greatly improved. It could be found that with the increase of CDs content from 1 wt% to 3 wt%, the CIP degradation efficiency presented an upward trend, which could reach up to 90%. However, when CDs content increased to 5 wt%, the degradation efficiency of CIP decreased; for one thing, excessive load might inhibit the degradation of CIP, and for another, it was possible to deteriorate the charge transfer channel, which resulted in a decrease of separation efficiency. Interestingly, the CIP degradation efficiency of Bi4O5Br2/ATP and 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP under visible light irradiation were obviously weaker than those of simulated sunlight. Thus, the increased photocatalytic activity of CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP sample might be owing to the compact contacted heterostructure that promoted charge separation. The decrease in sample activity might be related to the excessive CDs accumulation on the catalyst surface [38].

As illustrated in Fig. 7 (b) and Table S1, corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetic curves and data show that 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP has a maximum kinetic constant (K = 0.0178 min−1), which is 6.77 times that of pure Bi4O5Br2 (K = 0.00263 min−1).

The cyclic property and structural stability of the prepared materials are important factors in the measurement of it for further practical application. A cyclic experiment of photocatalytic degradation of CIP was performed under simulated sunlight to investigate the structural stability of CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite material. After four cycles, nearly no reduction in photocatalytic capability was detected in Fig. 8 (a), demonstrating that CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composites had superior stability in eliminating pollutants. XRD patterns of the CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite (Fig. 8 (b)) were not significantly altered after cycling use. It was proven that the structure of the CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite was still stable and intact through cyclic experiment. Therefore, the CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite has not only excellent photocatalytic performance but also high stability, which is supposed for further application.

Fluorescence spectra is shown in Fig. 9 . The charge transfer and recombination behavior of the synthesized sample was studied by photoluminescence (PL) experiments. A low photoluminescence intensity, in general, suggests a low photogenerated carrier recombination rate [44]. The spectrum intensity of Bi4O5Br2/ATP dropped relative to that of Bi4O5Br2 and ATP, showing that the composite material formed heterojunction and lowered the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers. The spectrum intensity of 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP dropped when compared to Bi4O5Br2/ATP, indicating that the Z-type heterojunction was created after doping CDs and the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers was lowered. In other words, composites prevented photoinduced carrier recombination thus increasing the photocatalytic activity of the composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Active species capture experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) detection
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 (a) depicts the capture of active species in a photocatalytic reaction of 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP under visible light irradiation. Adding 1 mM t-BuOH (·OH quenching agent) and 1 mM BQ (·O2 – quenching agent) had no effect on CIP photocatalytic degradation, however adding 1 mM EDTA-2Na (h+ quenching agent) dramatically decreased CIP photocatalytic degradation. As a result, it is possible that h+ is the most active chemical for CIP degradation in visible light.

To establish the presence of ·O2 – and ·OH or not, an active species detection test was performed on CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP by ESR under visible light irradiation. As illustrated in Fig. 10 (b), it was discovered that in the dark, neither signal was observed in the ESR spectra. There were essentially no distinctive ·OH and ·O2 – peaks after 10 min of irradiation on a 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP sample, which was consistent with the free radical capture experiment. It could be reasonably speculated that h+ was the active substance for CIP degradation when 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP was irradiated with visible light.

Different free radical capture tests were performed under simulated sunlight to further establish the effect of 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP active species in photocatalytic degradation. As shown in Fig. 11 (a), the traps for ·O2 –, h+, and ·OH were benzoquinone (BQ), EDTA-2Na, and t-BuOH, respectively. When BQ was added to the solution, very minimal photocatalytic degradation efficiency was measured, indicating that ·O2 – was the primary active species. When EDTA-2Na was added to the solution, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency was reduced by 70%, demonstrating that h+ was also involved in the degradation process. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency was considerably lowered when t-BuOH was added to the solution.

To further validate the foregoing results and establish the existence of ·O2 – and ·OH or not, an active species detection test was performed on CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP by ESR. As illustrated in Fig. 11 (b), it was discovered that neither signal was observed in the ESR spectra in the dark, but the typical peaks of ·OH and ·O2 – were clearly visible after 10 min of illumination of a 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP sample. It is reasonable to assume that the Z-scheme heterojunction effectively utilized electrons to participate in the reduction of O2 and h+ was also effectively utilized to participate in the oxidation of H2O, indicating that ·O2 –, h+, and ·OH played a coordinating role in the degradation of pollutants under simulated sunlight irradiation and 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP had excellent photocatalytic activity.

The valence bands of ATP and Bi4O5Br2 were measured by XPS valence band spectrum, which were 3.1 eV and 1.96 eV respectively (Fig. S2). According to ECB = EVB − Eg, the conduction bands of ATP and Bi4O5Br2 were −0.15 eV and −0.36 eV respectively. Fig. 12 (a) depicts the photocatalytic mechanism of CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composites under visible light, whereas Fig. 12 (b) and (c) depict the photocatalytic mechanism of simulated sunlight. When just visible light was utilized for irradiation, Bi4O5Br2 was excited to create electrons and holes, but ATP was not excited, as seen in Fig. 12 (a). The electrons of Bi4O5Br2 were transferred to CDs and then to ATP when CDs was employed to alter the Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite. However, because −0.15 eV > -0.33 eV, the electrons transferred to ATP were unable to create ·O2 – [26,45].When simulated sunlight was irradiated, electrons of both ATP and Bi4O5Br2 could be stimulated, as illustrated in Fig. 12 (b). Because −0.36 eV was more negative than −0.15 eV, electrons could be transmitted to ATP through CDs, and 3.1 eV was greater than 1.96 eV, holes could be carried from ATP to Bi4O5Br2. The electrons transported to ATP, however, could not form ·O2 –, and the degradation effect should be identical to that of visible light irradiation. However, the result in Fig. 7 is clearly contradictory with this, hence the following process is postulated. As demonstrated in Fig. 12 (c), electrons transferred from CB of ATP to VB of Bi4O5Br2, and e- of Bi4O5Br2 and h+ of ATP accumulate in situ. Because −0.36 eV was more negative than −0.33 eV, ·O2 – was produced on the CB of Bi4O5Br2. Because of 3.1 eV > 1.99 eV, ATP could produce ·OH, the electron transfer pathway of the Z mechanism in Fig. 12 (c) was determined. As a result, CDs were critical in successfully separating photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the Z-scheme heterojunction, so CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP exhibited good photocatalytic activity in the degradation of CIP aqueous solution under simulated sunlight. In general, photocatalytic oxidation removal of CIP might be linked to several active molecules such as ·O2 –, h+, and ·OH. These active molecules, which had a high oxidation capacity, could oxidize water adsorbed on the surface of ATP and react with the pollutant to form the final innocuous product. According to the findings, CIP removal in the photocatalytic oxidation reaction might include the following reaction [46,47,48]:
CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP + hv → ATP (h+) + Bi4O5Br2 (e−) (1)
O2 + e−→·O2 – (2)
·O2 –+CIP → CO2 + H2O (3)
H2O + h+→·OH + H+ (4)
OH−+h+→·OH (5)
·OH + CIP → CO2 + H2O (6)

The great light responsive Z-scheme CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP composite photocatalytic material was successfully synthesized by the dynamic adsorption method in this study. The Z-scheme heterojunction was generated due to a favorable energy band structure between CDs, Bi4O5Br2 and ATP, which dramatically lowered the photoexcited electron-hole pairs recombination rate and greatly enhanced the degrading efficiency of ciprofloxacin. The experimental results of photocatalytic degradation of CIP revealed that simulated sunlight irradiation was superior to visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic activity of CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP was optimum when the CDs doping mass was 3%. Through the analysis of the energy band structure, the possible electron conduction paths were speculated. On this basis, combining the free radical trapping experiment and ESR test, it was revealed that the active species of the CDs/Bi4O5Br2/ATP photocatalyst were superoxide radical and hydroxyl radical, which played a significant role in the degradation process. Thus, this study not only demonstrates a method for manufacturing novel Z-scheme heterojunction, but also contributes to future research on antibiotic elimination in aquatic environments.
Jing Lu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yaqian Zhang: Data curation, Investigation. Bowen Wang: Data curation, Investigation. Fenge Zhang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Nos. 41772240). The authors would like to thank Chen Mengyan from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.110759.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
